title: Willow flat-back wall basket
subtitle: A D-shaped wall-mounted basket in buff willow for herbs, letters, or kitchen storage
excerpt: A flat-back basket has a D-shaped plan: a woven front face, a flat back panel, and an open top. Mounted on a wall, it holds herbs, letters, secateurs, or anything that needs to be off a worktop and within reach. The flat back is built as a panel frame rather than a base, which makes the construction process distinct from a round or oval basket. This intermediate project covers the frame, the front face, and the looped hanging rod.
sourceNotes: Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for the flat-back basket form and its domestic uses. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for frame-basket construction.

content.text: A flat-back basket is built around a rigid back frame rather than a woven base. The frame gives the D-shape and carries the hanging rod; the woven face is built outward from the frame in
soaked overnight for all weaving rods. The thicker frame and handle rods need 4 to 6 hours longer soaking to bend without cracking.

For a 30 cm tall, 25 cm wide basket: 2 thick rods for the back frame (70 cm long, 8 mm diameter), 1 hanging rod (30 cm long, 8 mm diameter), 16 to 20 stakes (50 cm long, 4 to 5 mm diameter), 40 to 60 weaving rods (50 to 70 cm long, 3 to 4 mm diameter), all in buff willow. Soak overnight.
A rapping iron to pack weavers after each row. A sloyd knife to point stakes and trim rod ends.
Point rod ends away from the body when cutting. Keep the free hand above the rapping iron's strike zone.
Step 1: Bend and tie the back frame

Bend the two frame rods into a D-shape; their ends meet at the bottom. Hold the two bent rods together with the hanging rod lashed across the top of the D using a figure-of-eight wrap. The frame is now a rigid D-shape with a projecting loop at the top for wall hanging. Allow the frame to dry in this shape before weaving begins; this keeps the back flat.

Thread the pointed end of each stake through the lashing at the frame base, spacing them evenly across the width. Fold stakes upward to stand perpendicular to the back frame, forming the uprights for the front face. Weave a four-rod wale immediately above the base lashing to lock the stakes in position.

Insert a weaving rod behind any stake. Rand from left to right across the front face (in front of one stake, behind the next) to the end stake, then turn and continue back in the opposite direction. This builds a flat woven face. Pack down with the rapping iron after every two rows. Add a wale every 5 cm of height. Continue until the basket is 20 cm deep from the hanging rod.

Bend each upright stake down in turn and weave the four-rod border along the front rim: each stake goes behind the stake to its right, in front of the next two, and is tucked down. Trim all projecting rod ends flush with the sloyd knife.
A double-pocket version adds a horizontal divider rod tied across the middle of the frame. Stakes on the lower half make a second, smaller pocket below.
Brown willow (unstripped dried rods) gives a darker, more rustic surface than buff willow and is traditional for outdoor garden baskets.
Wipe the basket with a damp cloth occasionally. Do not saturate with water when in use as a kitchen basket; allow it to dry out between heavy use.

items.fix: Pack each row more firmly with the rapping iron in the centre than at the edges to compensate. On future baskets, consciously ease the tension on the outer stakes at the turn.
items.cause: Weaving tension was too tight on the outer stakes and too loose in the centre.
items.symptom: The front face bows forward rather than lying flat

items.fix: Add an extra lashing turn at the base frame join. Ensure the first wale is woven with firm, even tension before adding further rows.
items.cause: The frame lashing at the base was not tight enough, or the stakes were not locked in with the first wale.
items.symptom: The D-frame opens out during weaving
